title: The more I learn about trading, the more I realize it’s about saying no
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
Hey everyone,

Something that’s been becoming clearer over time:

Trading isn’t really about finding more opportunities.  

It’s about rejecting most of them.

At first, everything looks tradable:

\- setups seem valid  

\- levels look clean  

\- there’s always a reason to enter  

But with more experience, you start noticing that:

\- a lot of setups are just “almost right”  

\- conditions aren’t always aligned  

\- and forcing trades is where most losses come from  

So instead of asking:

👉 “is this a good trade?”  

I’ve been asking:

👉 “is there any reason I shouldn’t take this?”

If the answer isn’t a clear no, I usually stay out.

It’s a simple shift, but it filters out a lot.

Curious how you approach it:

👉 Do you focus more on finding trades, or filtering them?

comments.author: Far-Photograph-2342
comments.body: Now I’m much more focused on *not trading* unless everything is really aligned. Most of the damage I did early on came from “it looks fine enough” entries, not obvious bad setups.

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
That’s exactly it.

The obvious bad setups are rarely the problem most people can spot those.

It’s the “looks fine enough” trades that do the damage, because they pass just enough checks to feel justified.

Shifting to “everything needs to be aligned” changes the game completely.

You end up trading less, but your decisions become much cleaner and that’s where consistency really starts showing up.
